questions.a: An execution path within a process that shares memory with its peers.

questions.q: What is a mutex used for?
questions.a: Mutual exclusion around a critical section.
A counter-based synchronization primitive used to control access to shared resources.
A state where threads wait forever for each other to release resources.
A bug where the result depends on the timing or interleaving of concurrent operations.
A block of code that must not be executed by more than one thread at a time.

questions.q: Why does shared mutable state make concurrency hard?
questions.a: Multiple threads can observe and modify the same data in inconsistent orders.

questions.q: Why can lock contention hurt throughput?
questions.a: Threads spend time waiting instead of doing useful work, so parallelism drops.
Threads keep reacting to each other but never make forward progress.
They let threads update shared state without an intermediate inconsistent state.

questions.q: What is the difference between a mutex and a read-write lock?
questions.a: A mutex allows one holder; a read-write lock can allow many readers or one writer.
They let threads sleep until a condition becomes true without busy waiting.

questions.q: When would you choose threads over processes?
questions.a: When you want shared memory, low overhead, and the workload is mostly in-process coordination.

questions.q: What tradeoff does fine-grained locking introduce?
questions.a: It improves concurrency, but increases code complexity and can raise deadlock risk.

questions.q: When is lock-free programming worth the effort?
questions.a: When contention is high enough that lock overhead is the bottleneck and correctness can still be proven.

questions.q: Why can async I/O be better than a thread per connection?
questions.a: It avoids the memory and scheduling cost of many blocked threads.

questions.q: What tradeoff does a larger thread pool introduce?
questions.a: It can absorb bursts better, but too many workers increase contention and context switching.

questions.q: Why is reducing lock scope usually good practice?
questions.a: Shorter critical sections reduce waiting time and improve concurrency.
